mission.title: Install and Configure Envoy on Kubernetes
mission.description: Envoy is a cloud-native high-performance edge, middle, and service proxy that helps manage communication between microservices. Installing Envoy allows you to leverage its advanced features for load balancing, service discovery, and observability in your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the Envoy Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Envoy Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add envoy https://envoyproxy.github.io/envoy
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Envoy using Helm
steps.description:
Install Envoy in a new namespace called 'envoy-system' with the following command:
```bash
helm install envoy envoy/envoy --namespace envoy-system --create-namespace --version v1.37.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Envoy pods are running successfully with the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace envoy-system
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Update the Envoy deployment to set resource limits. Use the following command to edit the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment envoy --namespace envoy-system
```
In the editor, add the following under 'spec.containers.resources':
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
To access the Envoy service, set up port forwarding with the following command:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/envoy --namespace envoy-system 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Envoy will show the Envoy pods in the 'envoy-system' namespace as running. You can access the Envoy service through port 8080 on your local machine.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add envoy https://envoyproxy.github.io/envoy
helm repo update
helm install envoy envoy/envoy --namespace envoy-system --create-namespace --version v1.37.0

resources:

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have installed Helm and kubectl on your local machine.
